comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a principled framework that derives recurrent neural modules for sequences and graphs from classical kernel constructions (string kernels and graph kernels such as random-walk and Weisfeiler–Lehman). The authors show that the internal states of the proposed RNN-style modules correspond to inner products between the input and parameterized “reference” structures and formally characterize the induced RKHS for single-layer and deep (stacked) variants. Extensions include gated adaptive decay and connections to WL relabeling. Empirical evaluations on language modeling (PTB), sentence sentiment classification (SST), and molecular property regression (Harvard Clean Energy Project) are reported and claim competitive or state-of-the-art performance against relevant baselines.

Strengths:
- Principled contribution: The paper provides a clean, conceptually appealing connection between structured kernels and neural architectures. Framing RNN/graph modules as parameterized kernel inner products gives theoretical grounding to architecture design.
- Theoretical characterization: The authors formalize single-layer and deep constructions and argue that the post-activation states lie in the RKHS of composed kernels. The extension to graphs (random-walk, unified local kernels, WL) is useful and non-trivial.
- Practical instantiations and extensions: The paper proposes workable network modules (sequence and graph) including gated adaptive decay, and relates its modules to known graph procedures (Weisfeiler-Lehman), which helps interpretability and potential adoption.
- Empirical evidence: Experiments cover several important tasks (language modeling, sentiment, molecular regression) and show competitive results compared to strong baselines; ablations (e.g., gating, λ variants) are presented for sequences.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- Some important assumptions and limitations are not emphasized enough. A key technical assumption is that certain activations lie in the RKHS of some kernel (Lemma 1). This holds for some activations but not all, and the practical implications (which activations are allowed, approximation issues for infinite-dimensional feature maps) should be stated more explicitly.
- Proofs and many technical details are deferred to the supplementary material; the main text sometimes glosses over technical conditions (boundedness, existence of mappings of possibly infinite dimension). While not fatal, it limits the immediate verifiability of claims in the main paper.
- Experiments could include stronger ablations. For instance, a direct parameter-matched comparison to LSTM/GRU/other gated graph networks across all tasks would help isolate whether the kernel-derived architecture yields systematic gains or whether gains are due to other design/hyperparameter choices. Likewise, more ablations on the effect of n (n-gram width), depth L, and the trade-off between width/depth would be informative.
- In the molecular regression experiment, the paper follows the resampling strategy of prior work to emphasize rare/high-PCE molecules. This should be discussed explicitly in terms of how it affects comparability and whether the reported RMSE is directly comparable to baselines that may not use the same resampling.
- Practical considerations such as computational cost, scalability (especially for higher-order n or deep stacking), and memory implications of the proposed modules are not thoroughly discussed.

Suggestions for improvement / Follow-up experiments:
- Provide more explicit statements (or an appendix paragraph) about which activation functions satisfy the RKHS requirement and comment on practical consequences when using standard activations (ReLU, tanh, sigmoid, polynomial approximations).
- Include parameter-matched baselines (same number of parameters and similar optimization/regularization) for LSTM/GRU and graph baselines, plus wall-clock or FLOP comparisons to demonstrate efficiency trade-offs.
- Ablate key design choices more thoroughly: gated vs constant λ, different n values, different compositions (additive vs multiplicative), and effect of parameter tying across layers (WL-style tying) on both performance and parameter efficiency.
- Add qualitative analysis: visualize / inspect learned reference patterns (rows of W matrices interpreted as reference n-grams or walks) to validate the interpretation and aid interpretability.
- Clarify details of dataset preprocessing (resampling) and ensure consistent reporting compared to baselines; if different, report both resampled and raw-evaluation metrics.

Overall assessment:
This is a well-motivated and original contribution that bridges kernel methods and neural-network architecture design for structured data. The theoretical grounding and the concrete module designs are likely to be of interest to researchers working on sequence and graph representation learning. With some additional clarification of assumptions and more extensive ablations/comparisons, the empirical claims would be further strengthened. I recommend acceptance (strong positive).
